source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI key file permissions are set to 600

input_row.source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI key file permissions are set to 600
input_row.description: Ensure	that	Kubernetes	PKI	key	files	have	permissions	of	600.
input_row.rationale: Kubernetes	makes	use	of	a	number	of	key	files	as	part	of	the	operation	of	its	components. The	permissions	on	these	files	should	be	set	to	600	to	protect	their	integrity	and confidentiality. Impact: None
input_row.audit: Run	the	below	command	(based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system)	on	the	master	node. For	example, ls -laR /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.key Verify	that	the	permissions	are	600.
input_row.remediation: Run	the	below	command	(based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system)	on	the	master	node. For	example, chmod -R 600 /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.key Default	Value: By	default,	the	keys	used	by	Kubernetes	are	set	to	have	permissions	of	600 References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/ 56	|	P a g e Additional	Information: This	control	cannot	be	audited	and	modified	in	ACK	managed	clusters. CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 57	|	P a g e 1.2 API Server This	section	contains	recommendations	relating	to	API	server	configuration	flags

Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 57	|	P a g e 1.2 API Server This	section	contains	recommendations	relating	to	API	server	configuration	flags
input_row.justification: This control requires verification of file permissions on Kubernetes PKI key files located on the master node’s file system. Alibaba Cloud APIs, CLI, and managed ACK service do not expose file-level permission data for these nodes, nor do they provide a mechanism to remotely audit or enforce these permissions through cloud-native tools. Both AI opinions acknowledge that direct OS-level access (typically via SSH) is required, and while automation via scripts is theoretically possible, it still fundamentally relies on manual or privileged access outside Alibaba Cloud’s API surface.
input_row.manual_summary: 1. SSH into the Kubernetes master node(s) where the PKI key files are stored. 2. Navigate to the PKI directory, typically `/etc/kubernetes/pki/`. 3. Run the command: `ls -laR /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.key` 4. Review the output and verify that each `.key` file has permissions set to `-rw-------` (600). 5. Document any files that do not meet this requirement and remediate as necessary by running `chmod 600 <filename>`. 6. Confirm compliance by re-running the `ls -laR` command and ensuring all files now have the correct permissions.
input_row.default_value: By default, the keys used by Kubernetes are set to have permissions of 600
input_row.additional_information: This control cannot be audited and modified in ACK managed clusters.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control mandates ensuring that the Kubernetes PKI key files have permissions set to 600 on the master node. The control is not auditable through Alibaba Cloud's APIs, Aliyun CLI, or Alibaba Cloud CLI because it specifically involves checking file permissions at the operating system level of the Kubernetes master node. The CIS benchmark specifies that this control cannot be modified or audited in ACK managed clusters. Verifying file permissions requires direct SSH access to the Kubernetes master node, and therefore, this control cannot be automated.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into the Kubernetes master node(s) where the PKI key files are stored.
2. Navigate to the PKI directory, typically `/etc/kubernetes/pki/`.
3. Run the command: `ls -laR /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.key`.
4. Review the output and verify that each `.key` file has permissions set to `-rw-------` (600).
5. Document any files that do not meet this requirement and remediate as necessary by running `chmod 600 <filename>`.
6. Confirm compliance by re-running the `ls -laR` command and ensuring all files now have the correct permissions.
